steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question testing punctuation in a complex series. The sentence lists three of Carla Hayden’s responsibilities. Because the first two items each contain internal commas (making them complex), the items must be separated by semicolons — not commas — to prevent the reader from confusing item boundaries. The blank separates item 1 (“overseeing…162 million items”) from item 2 (“managing…copyright law”).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘items managing’: no punctuation between items 1 and 2 fuses them into one run-on phrase, making the series impossible to parse. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘items, managing’: a comma here conflicts with the semicolon already used later to separate items 2 and 3 (“managing…law; and appointing…”). Mixing commas and semicolons as separators in the same series is not standard. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘items; managing’: a semicolon correctly separates item 1 from item 2, matching the semicolon that separates items 2 and 3 later in the sentence. This creates a consistent, readable complex series. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘items. Managing’: a period ends the sentence after item 1, leaving “Managing…copyright law; and appointing the US poet laureate” as a grammatically unacceptable sentence fragment. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
